name: Eric Zhang
bio:
Computer systems person, interaction designer. @thinkymachines
prev founding eng @modal

→ dreams of: a simpler, more honest, more human sort of software

tweets.text: Thanks Bryce @blelbach for coming to NYSRG and giving a really wonderful talk about cuTile—also just being incredibly knowledgeable about everything GPUs, from scientific computing to ML. feeling lucky to get to hang out + learn a lot :) https://t.co/3j6q0nWclk

Being mildly sick is like, you wake up and feel awful, uh oh. So you stop doing things, and at some point it stops getting worse. And there's this empty space… just your thoughts, and then you finally recall the last time you've been idle and realize how much you've sacrificed
honestly just "phil's thoughts" sounds pretty great too
we’ll know for sure that the ai is superintelligent when it can correctly do tasks that no human can, such as set IAM policies correctly

5 years ago, I was making programming languages, dreaming up interfaces, learning graphics, building with Rust/Wasm.

everything was new. how beautiful were the things you made if you put yourself out there and were a bit scrappy?

then at some point I GOT A JOB and started doing INFRA and working with COMPLEX CODEBASES and PRODUCTION SERVICES. and now I am like every other software engineer. lol

today I really appreciate the engineering behind OSS AI infra, like sglang/vllm.

Like yes, they're huge messes of complexity, but they also "just work" out-of-the-box for tons of people, hundreds of contributors, various setups—kind of amazing. It must take so much work

For the next couple weeks at NY systems reading group, we’ll be writing some GPU kernels

This was much requested, and I think now that there’s some excellent, well-written content online about CuTe DSL, makes sense to learn!

(CUDA is too hard / arcane, CuTe DSL is just low-level enough to be interesting to systems folks looking for a new challenge perhaps. Plus it might be useful to learn it for work.)

I was trying to figure out how we give people a quick trial of Hopper/Blackwell GPUs and realized I already made a free product for this at my last job lol, and it’s on-demand + collaborative. So we’ll use Notebooks.

https://t.co/ArFSF8ESxA
it really doesn’t feel like mid-February
RT @bilaltwovec: very belated update: i joined @isomorphiclabs right after graduating early last year—its been really incredible  getting t…

one thing I realized is that for many of these math+coding benchmarks, there’s really not that many problems. AIME is only 15 questions, and TerminalBench is <100, written by engineers — they sometimes give the impression of being larger
